exercises.name: Barbell Back Squat
exercises.aliases: back squat|bb squat|low bar squat|high bar squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Set the bar across your upper traps, unrack and step back with feet shoulder-width apart.
2. Brace your core, push your hips back and bend your knees.
3. Lower until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
4. Drive through your whole foot to stand back up.
exercises.tips:
• Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout.
• Don't let your knees cave inward — push them out over your toes.
• Inhale and brace before descending; exhale at the top.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Squat
exercises.aliases: front squat|clean grip squat|cross arm squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Rest the bar on your front delts with a clean grip or crossed-arm grip.
2. Keep elbows high, unrack and step back.
3. Squat down keeping an upright torso until thighs are parallel.
4. Drive up through your heels to return to standing.
exercises.tips:
• High elbows are critical — if they drop, the bar rolls forward.
• This variation emphasizes quads more than back squats.
• Work on wrist/lat mobility if the clean grip feels uncomfortable.

exercises.name: Safety Bar Squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Place the safety squat bar on your traps with handles in front.
2. Grip the handles, unrack and step back.
3. Squat down keeping your torso upright.
4. Drive up through your feet to stand.
exercises.tips:
• The bar naturally pushes you forward — fight to stay upright.
• Great for those with shoulder mobility issues.
• Use handles to push up and assist if needed.

exercises.name: Zercher Squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Hold the bar in the crooks of your elbows close to your body.
2. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
3. Squat down keeping the bar tight to your torso.
4. Drive up through your heels to return.
exercises.tips:
• Use a pad or towel around the bar to protect your arms.
• Keeps you very upright — great for core and quad engagement.
• Start light to get used to the position.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Position the bar across your upper back under the smith machine.
2. Place feet slightly in front of your hips.
3. Unrack and lower until thighs are parallel.
4. Press up to return to the start.
exercises.tips:
• Placing feet forward allows you to sit back more safely.
• The fixed bar path removes the need for stabilization.
• Good for beginners or high-rep burnout sets.

exercises.name: Hack Squat Machine
exercises.instructions:
1. Position your back flat against the pad, shoulders under the pads.
2. Place feet shoulder-width on the platform.
3. Release the safety handles and lower until your knees are at 90°.
4. Press through your feet to extend your legs.
exercises.tips:
• Foot placement changes emphasis — higher = more glutes, lower = more quads.
• Don't lock out knees fully at the top.
• Keep your lower back pressed into the pad.

exercises.name: Pendulum Squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Step onto the platform and position your shoulders under the pads.
2. Release the safety, keep your back against the pad.
3. Lower by bending your knees until you reach full depth.
4. Push through your feet to extend.
exercises.tips:
• The arc motion keeps constant tension on the quads.
• Great for isolating quads with less spinal load.
• Control the negative — don't drop into the bottom.

exercises.name: Belt Squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Attach the belt around your hips and stand on the platforms.
2. Stand tall with arms free or holding handles for balance.
3. Squat down until thighs are parallel.
4. Drive up to standing.
exercises.tips:
• Zero spinal loading — excellent for back-friendly leg training.
• Keep an upright posture throughout.
• Vary stance width to change muscle emphasis.

exercises.name: Goblet Squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Hold a dumbbell or kettlebell at chest height with both hands.
2. Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width.
3. Squat down keeping the weight close to your chest, elbows inside your knees.
4. Drive up to return to standing.
exercises.tips:
• The front-loaded position naturally keeps you upright.
• Great warm-up exercise or for higher rep ranges.
• Push your knees out with your elbows at the bottom.

exercises.name: Leg Press 45
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit in the machine with your back flat against the pad.
2. Place feet shoulder-width on the platform.
3. Release the safety, lower the weight by bending your knees to about 90°.
4. Press through your feet to extend without locking out.
exercises.tips:
• Don't let your lower back lift off the pad at the bottom.
• Higher foot placement shifts emphasis to glutes/hamstrings.
• Use a controlled tempo — don't bounce at the bottom.

exercises.name: Horizontal Leg Press
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit in the machine and place feet on the platform.
2. Push to release the weight, then bend knees toward your chest.
3. Lower until knees form about 90°.
4. Press back to the start without full lockout.
exercises.tips:
• Keep your head back and core braced.
• Similar principles to 45° press — adjust foot position for emphasis.
• Great for high-volume quad work with less fatigue.

exercises.name: Single Leg Press
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit in the leg press and place one foot centered on the platform.
2. Release the safety locks.
3. Lower the weight by bending your knee to about 90°.
4. Press through your foot to extend. Repeat on the other side.
exercises.tips:
• Use lighter weight than bilateral pressing.
• Helps identify and correct strength imbalances.
• Keep your hips square — don't rotate.

exercises.name: Leg Extension
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit in the machine with the pad resting on top of your ankles.
2. Adjust the back pad so your knees align with the machine pivot.
3. Extend your legs fully, squeezing your quads at the top.
4. Lower slowly under control.
exercises.tips:
• Squeeze and hold at the top for 1 second for maximum contraction.
• Don't use momentum to swing the weight up.
• Point your toes up (dorsiflexion) to increase quad activation.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift
exercises.instructions:
1. Hold a barbell at hip height with an overhand grip.
2. Push your hips back, letting the bar slide down your thighs.
3. Lower until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings (mid-shin).
4. Drive your hips forward to return to the top.
exercises.tips:
• Keep a slight bend in your knees — this isn't a stiff-leg deadlift.
• The bar should stay close to your body the entire time.
• Think "hips back" not "bending over."

exercises.name: Stiff Leg Deadlift
exercises.instructions:
1. Hold a barbell with an overhand grip, legs nearly straight.
2. Hinge at the hips and lower the bar toward your feet.
3. Go as low as your hamstring flexibility allows.
4. Return to standing by extending your hips.
exercises.tips:
• Legs stay straighter than an RDL — more hamstring stretch.
• Keep a neutral spine throughout.
• Focus on feeling the stretch in the back of your legs.

exercises.name: Conventional Deadlift
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand with feet hip-width, bar over mid-foot.
2. Bend down, grip the bar just outside your legs.
3. Brace your core, chest up, pull the slack out of the bar.
4. Drive through your feet to stand up, locking out hips and knees together.
exercises.tips:
• The bar should travel in a straight vertical line.
• Don't round your lower back — keep it neutral.
• Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up.

exercises.name: Sumo Deadlift
exercises.instructions:
1. Take a wide stance with toes pointed out 30–45°.
2. Grip the bar inside your legs with arms straight down.
3. Drop your hips, chest up, and brace your core.
4. Drive through your feet, pushing your knees out, to stand up.
exercises.tips:
• This shifts more emphasis to quads, glutes and adductors.
• Hips should be closer to the bar than in conventional.
• Let the knees track over the toes throughout.

exercises.name: Trap Bar Deadlift
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand inside the trap bar with feet hip-width.
2. Bend down and grip the handles.
3. Brace and drive through your feet to stand up.
4. Lower under control back to the floor.
exercises.tips:
• More quad-friendly than conventional deadlifts.
• The neutral grip reduces stress on the lower back.
• Great for beginners learning the hip hinge.

exercises.name: Lying Leg Curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Lie face down on the machine with the pad behind your ankles.
2. Adjust so your knees align with the machine pivot point.
3. Curl your heels toward your glutes, squeezing at the top.
4. Lower slowly under control.
exercises.tips:
• Don't lift your hips off the pad — keep them pressed down.
• Squeeze for a full second at the top.
• Avoid using momentum to swing the weight.

exercises.name: Seated Leg Curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit in the machine with the pad above your heels and thigh pad secured.
2. Curl your heels under the seat by bending your knees.
3. Squeeze at the bottom of the movement.
4. Return slowly to the starting position.
exercises.tips:
• The seated position stretches the hamstrings more at the top.
• Use a slow eccentric (3 seconds) for more growth stimulus.
• Don't fully straighten your legs at the top — keep tension.

exercises.name: Nordic Curl
exercises.aliases: nordic ham|natural glute ham|nordic hamstring curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Kneel on a pad with your ankles locked under a pad or held by a partner.
2. Keep your body straight from knees to shoulders.
3. Slowly lower yourself forward under control as far as you can.
4. Use your hamstrings to pull yourself back up (or push off the floor to assist).
exercises.tips:
• Extremely effective but difficult — use a band for assistance if needed.
• Focus on the slow eccentric (lowering) if you can't do the concentric yet.
• Keep your hips extended — don't bend at the waist.

exercises.name: Good Morning
exercises.instructions:
1. Place a barbell across your upper back like a squat.
2. With a slight knee bend, hinge forward at the hips.
3. Lower your torso until nearly parallel to the floor.
4. Return to upright by driving your hips forward.
exercises.tips:
• Start very light — this is a challenging movement.
• Keep the weight on your heels.
• Think of it as an RDL with the bar on your back.

exercises.name: Barbell Hip Thrust
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit on the floor with your upper back against a bench, barbell over your hips.
2. Roll the bar into position over your hip crease (use a pad).
3. Drive through your heels to lift your hips until your body is flat from knees to shoulders.
4. Squeeze your glutes hard at the top, then lower under control.
exercises.tips:
• Chin should be slightly tucked — look forward, not at the ceiling.
• Your shins should be vertical at the top.
• Use a thick barbell pad to avoid hip bruising.

exercises.name: Machine Hip Thrust
exercises.instructions:
1. Set up in the hip thrust machine with your back against the pad.
2. Place feet flat on the platform about shoulder-width.
3. Drive through your heels to extend your hips fully.
4. Squeeze at the top and lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• The machine stabilizes everything so you can focus on squeezing.
• Experiment with foot position to find what you feel most in your glutes.
• Hold the top for 2 seconds for extra activation.

exercises.name: Cable Kickback
exercises.instructions:
1. Attach an ankle cuff to a low cable, face the machine.
2. Stand on one leg, holding the frame for balance.
3. Kick the working leg straight back, squeezing your glute.
4. Return slowly and repeat. Switch legs.
exercises.tips:
• Keep your torso still — don't lean forward excessively.
• Focus on squeezing the glute, not swinging the leg.
• Slight forward lean can increase range of motion.

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand lunge-length in front of a bench, place your rear foot on the bench.
2. Hold dumbbells at your sides (or barbell on back).
3. Lower by bending your front knee until your thigh is parallel.
4. Drive through your front foot to stand back up.
exercises.tips:
• Keep your front knee tracking over your toes.
• Most of your weight should be on the front foot.
• The farther your front foot is from the bench, the more glute activation.

exercises.name: Walking Lunge
exercises.instructions:
1. Hold dumbbells at your sides, stand tall.
2. Step forward into a lunge, both knees bending to about 90°.
3. Push off your front foot and step your rear foot forward into the next lunge.
4. Continue alternating forward.
exercises.tips:
• Keep your torso upright throughout.
• Longer strides emphasize glutes; shorter strides emphasize quads.
• Lightly tap (don't slam) your rear knee toward the floor.

exercises.name: Step Up
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand facing a box or bench, holding dumbbells at your sides.
2. Place one foot fully on the box.
3. Drive through that foot to step up, bringing your other foot to the top.
4. Step back down with the trailing leg. Repeat.
exercises.tips:
• Don't push off with your back foot — let the working leg do all the work.
• Higher box = more glute/hamstring involvement.
• Keep your torso upright.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Raise
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand on the edge of the platform with the balls of your feet, shoulders under the pads.
2. Lower your heels as far as possible to get a deep stretch.
3. Rise up onto your toes as high as you can.
4. Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Full range of motion is key — stretch at the bottom, squeeze at the top.
• Use a 2-second pause at both the top and bottom.
• Keep your legs straight to emphasize the gastrocnemius.

exercises.name: Seated Calf Raise
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit in the machine with the pad over your lower thighs.
2. Place the balls of your feet on the platform edge.
3. Release the safety, lower your heels for a full stretch.
4. Raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing at the top.
exercises.tips:
• The bent-knee position targets the soleus more.
• Use slow, controlled reps — calves respond well to time under tension.
• 15-30 rep range tends to work best for calves.

exercises.name: Donkey Calf Raise
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand on the platform edge with your toes, bend forward at the hips.
2. Place the pad on your lower back/hips.
3. Lower your heels for a deep stretch.
4. Rise up onto your toes, squeezing at the top.
exercises.tips:
• The hip-flexed position provides a great stretch on the calves.
• If no machine is available, have a partner sit on your lower back.
• Use a full range of motion for best results.

exercises.name: Hip Abduction Machine
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit in the machine with your back against the pad and spine neutral.
2. Place your legs against the inner pads.
3. Push the pads apart by externally rotating your hips.
4. Slowly return to the starting position.
exercises.tips:
• Experiment with leaning forward slightly to change the emphasis.
• Don't let your lower back arch — keep your pelvis neutral.
• Use a controlled tempo, especially on the return.

exercises.name: Tibialis Raise
exercises.instructions:
1. Lean your back against a wall with feet about 2 feet in front.
2. Lift your toes up toward your shins.
3. Squeeze at the top.
4. Lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Strengthens the front of the shin — helps prevent shin splints.
• Can use a tib bar machine if available.
• High rep ranges (15-25) work well for this muscle.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.instructions:
1. Lie on the bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor.
2. Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width, unrack.
3. Lower the bar to your mid-chest with elbows at about 45°.
4. Press the bar up and slightly back to lockout.
exercises.tips:
• Retract and depress your shoulder blades — squeeze them together.
• Keep a slight arch in your lower back.
• Drive your feet into the floor for leg drive.

exercises.name: Incline Barbell Bench
exercises.instructions:
1. Set the bench to 30–45° incline.
2. Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width, unrack.
3. Lower the bar to your upper chest / collarbone area.
4. Press up to full lockout.
exercises.tips:
• Targets the upper chest more than flat bench.
• Don't set the incline too steep — 30° is often ideal.
• Keep your shoulder blades pinched back.

exercises.name: Decline Barbell Bench
exercises.instructions:
1. Set the bench to a slight decline and hook your feet.
2. Unrack the bar with a standard grip.
3. Lower to your lower chest.
4. Press back up to lockout.
exercises.tips:
• Emphasizes the lower chest fibers.
• Many people feel stronger in this position.
• Have a spotter hand off the bar for safety.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit on a flat bench with dumbbells on your thighs, kick them up as you lie back.
2. Hold the dumbbells above your chest with arms extended.
3. Lower the weights to the sides of your chest, elbows at ~45°.
4. Press back up, squeezing your chest at the top.
exercises.tips:
• Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion than barbell.
• Rotate your wrists slightly so pinkies come together at the top.
• Control the descent — don't let gravity pull the weights down.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Press
exercises.instructions:
1. Set bench to 30–45° incline, kick dumbbells up from your thighs.
2. Start with dumbbells above your upper chest, arms extended.
3. Lower to the sides of your upper chest.
4. Press up, squeezing at the top.
exercises.tips:
• Great for targeting upper chest with a deeper stretch.
• Keep elbows at about 45° to protect your shoulders.
• Don't arch too much at the incline angle.

exercises.name: Weighted Pushup
exercises.instructions:
1. Get in a plank position with a weight plate on your upper back (or weighted vest).
2. Hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
3. Lower your chest to the floor with elbows at ~45°.
4. Push back up to full extension.
exercises.tips:
• A partner can place/remove the plate for you.
• Keep your body in a straight line — don't sag your hips.
• A weighted vest is more practical for higher loads.

exercises.name: Weighted Decline Pushup
exercises.aliases: decline pushup|decline push up|weighted decline push up
exercises.instructions:
1. Place your feet on a bench/box with hands on the floor.
2. Add a weight plate on your back (or use a vest).
3. Lower your chest toward the floor.
4. Press back up to full extension.
exercises.tips:
• The decline angle mimics an incline press — targets upper chest.
• The higher the feet, the more shoulder involvement.
• Keep core tight to avoid sagging.

exercises.name: Pec Deck
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit in the machine with your back flat against the pad.
2. Place your forearms against the pads (or grip the handles).
3. Bring the pads together in front of your chest, squeezing.
4. Slowly return to the starting position with a stretch.
exercises.tips:
• Focus on the squeeze at the front for maximum contraction.
• Don't let the weight stack slam — control the eccentric.
• Keep a slight bend in your elbows.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.instructions:
1. Lie on a flat bench with dumbbells above your chest, palms facing each other.
2. With a slight bend in your elbows, lower the weights out to the sides.
3. Lower until you feel a stretch in your chest.
4. Squeeze your chest to bring the dumbbells back together.
exercises.tips:
• Think of hugging a large tree — the arc motion is key.
• Don't go too heavy — this is a stretch/squeeze exercise.
• Keep the slight elbow bend constant throughout.

exercises.name: Cable Fly Mid
exercises.instructions:
1. Set both cables to about shoulder height.
2. Stand in the center, grasp both handles, step forward slightly.
3. With elbows slightly bent, bring your hands together in front of your chest.
4. Slowly return to the stretched position.
exercises.tips:
• Constant cable tension throughout the entire range of motion.
• Cross your hands slightly at the front for a better squeeze.
• Great finisher exercise for the chest.

exercises.name: Cable Fly Low to High
exercises.instructions:
1. Set cables to the lowest position.
2. Grab handles and step forward, arms down at your sides.
3. Sweep your arms up and together in front of your upper chest.
4. Lower slowly back to the starting position.
exercises.tips:
• Targets the upper (clavicular) chest fibers.
• Don't shrug your shoulders — keep them down.
• A great alternative to incline flies.

exercises.name: Cable Fly High to Low
exercises.instructions:
1. Set cables to the highest position.
2. Grab handles, step forward, lean slightly.
3. Sweep your arms down and together in front of your lower chest.
4. Return slowly to the stretched position.
exercises.tips:
• Targets the lower chest fibers.
• Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout.
• Control the weight — don't let it snap back.

exercises.name: Dip
exercises.instructions:
1. Grip the parallel bars and lift yourself to full arm extension.
2. Lean slightly forward for chest emphasis.
3. Lower by bending your elbows until your shoulders are below your elbows.
4. Press back up to full lockout.
exercises.tips:
• Leaning forward emphasizes chest; staying upright targets triceps.
• Add weight with a dip belt once bodyweight becomes easy.
• Don't go so deep that your shoulders hurt.

exercises.name: Diamond Pushup
exercises.instructions:
1. Form a diamond shape with your index fingers and thumbs touching the floor.
2. Get into a push-up position with hands under your chest.
3. Lower your chest to your hands.
4. Press back up to full extension.
exercises.tips:
• Primarily targets the triceps with chest as secondary.
• Keep your elbows close to your body.
• Widen the diamond if wrist discomfort occurs.

exercises.name: Pull Up
exercises.instructions:
1. Hang from a bar with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width.
2. Engage your lats and pull your chest toward the bar.
3. Pull until your chin is over the bar.
4. Lower slowly to a full dead hang.
exercises.tips:
• Initiate the pull by depressing your shoulder blades, not bending your arms.
• Avoid kipping or swinging.
• Use an assisted machine or band if you can't do bodyweight yet.

exercises.name: Chin Up
exercises.instructions:
1. Hang from a bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, shoulder-width.
2. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar.
3. Squeeze your lats and biceps at the top.
4. Lower all the way to a dead hang.
exercises.tips:
• Easier than pull-ups due to more bicep involvement.
• Great for building both back and bicep size.
• Keep your core tight to prevent swinging.

exercises.name: Lat Pulldown
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit at the machine, grip the bar wider than shoulder-width.
2. Lean back slightly (~15°).
3. Pull the bar to your upper chest, squeezing your lats.
4. Return slowly with arms fully extended overhead.
exercises.tips:
• Drive your elbows down and back, not just pulling with your arms.
• Don't lean too far back — it turns into a row.
• Full stretch at the top is important for lat development.

exercises.name: Neutral Pulldown
exercises.instructions:
1. Attach a neutral grip (V-bar or close grip handle) to the cable.
2. Sit down and lean back slightly.
3. Pull the handle to your chest.
4. Extend your arms fully at the top.
exercises.tips:
• Neutral grip is often easier on the shoulders.
• Provides a longer range of motion than wide grip.
• Great for overall lat development.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.instructions:
1. Lie across a bench (just your upper back supported) holding one dumbbell overhead.
2. With slightly bent elbows, lower the dumbbell behind your head.
3. Stretch until you feel it in your lats and chest.
4. Pull the dumbbell back over your chest using your lats.
exercises.tips:
• Keep the elbow bend constant — don't turn it into a press.
• Drop your hips slightly to increase the stretch.
• Works both lats and chest — focus on your lats by thinking "elbows down."

exercises.name: Seated Cable Row
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit at the cable station, feet on the footplates, knees slightly bent.
2. Grab the handle with arms extended.
3. Pull the handle to your abdomen, squeezing your shoulder blades.
4. Extend your arms forward slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Keep upright — don't swing your torso back and forth.
• Full stretch at the front, full squeeze at the back.
• Try different attachments for variety (V-bar, wide bar, rope).

exercises.name: Chest Supported Row
exercises.instructions:
1. Set an incline bench to ~30–45°, lie face down with chest on the pad.
2. Hold dumbbells hanging straight down.
3. Row both dumbbells up, squeezing your shoulder blades.
4. Lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Eliminates momentum — pure back work.
• Great for those with lower back issues.
• Can also be done on a dedicated machine.

exercises.name: T Bar Row
exercises.instructions:
1. Straddle the T-bar or landmine setup.
2. Hinge forward and grip the handle.
3. Row the weight to your chest.
4. Lower under control.
exercises.tips:
• Allows heavier loads than most rowing variations.
• Keep your chest up and back neutral.
• A close grip targets the middle back more.

exercises.name: Barbell Row
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand with feet shoulder-width, hinge forward ~45° with a barbell hanging.
2. Grip slightly wider than shoulder-width, overhand or underhand.
3. Row the bar to your lower chest / upper abdomen.
4. Lower under control to full arm extension.
exercises.tips:
• Keep your back flat — don't round your spine.
• A little body English is fine on heavy sets, but don't make it a habit.
• Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top.

exercises.name: One Arm Dumbbell Row
exercises.instructions:
1. Place one knee and hand on a bench, other foot on the floor.
2. Hold a dumbbell in the free hand, arm hanging straight.
3. Row the dumbbell to your hip, driving your elbow back.
4. Lower slowly and repeat. Switch sides.
exercises.tips:
• Don't rotate your torso — keep your shoulders square.
• Drive your elbow past your torso for full contraction.
• Can also be done with one hand on a rack for support.

exercises.name: Face Pull
exercises.instructions:
1. Set a cable with a rope attachment at upper chest to face height.
2. Grab the rope with both hands, step back.
3. Pull toward your face, separating your hands and rotating externally.
4. Squeeze your rear delts, then slowly extend back.
exercises.tips:
• A must-do for shoulder health and posture.
• Think of pulling to your ears, not your chin.
• Keep elbows high — at or above shoulder height.

exercises.name: Reverse Pec Deck
exercises.aliases: reverse pec deck fly|rear delt machine
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit facing the pec deck machine, chest against the pad.
2. Grip the handles with your arms extended in front.
3. Pull the handles back in an arc, squeezing your rear delts.
4. Return slowly to the start.
exercises.tips:
• Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout.
• Don't use too much weight — focus on the squeeze.
• Great for rear delt development and shoulder balance.

exercises.name: Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand at a cable station with a bar or rope at the top.
2. With arms nearly straight, push the bar down in an arc to your thighs.
3. Squeeze your lats at the bottom.
4. Return slowly overhead.
exercises.tips:
• Keep just a slight bend in your elbows.
• Great for building mind-muscle connection with the lats.
• Use this as a warm-up or finisher.

exercises.name: Back Extension
exercises.instructions:
1. Position yourself on the back extension bench, hips on the pad.
2. Cross your arms over your chest or behind your head.
3. Lower your torso toward the floor.
4. Raise back up until your body is in a straight line.
exercises.tips:
• Don't hyperextend — stop when your body is in a straight line.
• Hold a weight plate at your chest for progressive overload.
• Squeeze your glutes at the top.

exercises.name: Reverse Hyper
exercises.instructions:
1. Lie face down on the reverse hyper bench, holding the handles.
2. Let your legs hang straight down.
3. Swing/raise your legs up until they're in line with your torso.
4. Lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Decompresses the spine while working the posterior chain.
• Don't swing excessively — use controlled momentum.
• Excellent for lower back rehab.

exercises.name: Overhead Press
exercises.aliases: ohp|military press|standing overhead press|sitting overhead press
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand or sit, unrack the barbell at collarbone height.
2. Brace your core and press the bar straight overhead.
3. Lock out with the bar over the back of your head.
4. Lower back to collarbone height under control.
exercises.tips:
• Move your head back slightly as the bar passes your face, then push it through.
• Squeeze your glutes to keep your lower back from arching.
• Full lockout overhead for maximum shoulder development.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit on a bench (90° or slight incline), dumbbells at shoulder height.
2. Press overhead until arms are fully extended.
3. Lower the weights to ear level.
4. Repeat.
exercises.tips:
• Dumbbells allow a more natural arc than a barbell.
• Don't let the dumbbells drift too far in front of you.
• Can also be done standing for more core engagement.

exercises.name: Arnold Press
exercises.instructions:
1. Start with dumbbells in front of your face, palms facing you.
2. Press up while rotating your palms to face forward.
3. Lock out overhead with palms forward.
4. Reverse the motion as you lower back down.
exercises.tips:
• The rotation hits all three delt heads through the movement.
• Use controlled rotation — don't rush it.
• Named after Arnold Schwarzenegger — a classic exercise.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand with dumbbells at your sides, slight bend in elbows.
2. Raise the dumbbells out to your sides until arms are parallel to the floor.
3. Pause briefly at the top.
4. Lower slowly under control.
exercises.tips:
• Lead with your elbows, not your hands.
• Don't shrug — keep your traps relaxed.
• A slight forward lean can help isolate the side delts more.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand sideways to a low cable, grab the handle with the far hand.
2. With a slight elbow bend, raise your arm out to the side to shoulder height.
3. Hold briefly at the top.
4. Lower slowly. Do all reps, then switch sides.
exercises.tips:
• Cable provides constant tension throughout the range.
• Stand upright — don't lean away to cheat the weight.
• Great for mind-muscle connection with the side delts.

exercises.name: Rear Delt Fly
exercises.instructions:
1. Hinge forward with dumbbells hanging below you, slight elbow bend.
2. Raise the dumbbells out to the sides, squeezing your rear delts.
3. Hold at the top briefly.
4. Lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Don't use momentum — keep the motion controlled.
• Think "pinch your shoulder blades" at the top.
• Can also be done seated on a bench for more stability.

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.instructions:
1. Hold a barbell at arm's length with an overhand grip.
2. Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears.
3. Hold the contraction for a second.
4. Lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Straight up and down — don't roll your shoulders.
• Use straps if your grip limits you.
• Can go heavy — traps respond well to load.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand with a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
2. Shrug your shoulders up as high as possible.
3. Hold and squeeze at the top.
4. Lower under control.
exercises.tips:
• Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion.
• Keep your arms straight — don't bend your elbows.
• Use a 2-second hold at the top for better activation.

exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand with feet shoulder-width, grip a barbell underhand at shoulder width.
2. Keep elbows pinned to your sides.
3. Curl the bar up to shoulder height, squeezing your biceps.
4. Lower slowly under control.
exercises.tips:
• Don't swing your body — keep it strict.
• Slight wrist supination at the top can improve the peak contraction.
• The straight bar hits both bicep heads well.

exercises.name: EZ Bar Curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Grip the EZ bar on the inner angled handles.
2. Stand upright, elbows at your sides.
3. Curl the bar up, squeezing at the top.
4. Lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Easier on the wrists than a straight bar.
• Don't let elbows drift forward — keep them pinned.
• Use the outer grip for more outer bicep emphasis.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand with a dumbbell in each hand, arms at your sides, palms forward.
2. Curl one or both dumbbells up toward your shoulders.
3. Squeeze at the top and supinate (twist pinky up) for maximum contraction.
4. Lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• Can be done alternating or simultaneously.
• Supination (twisting) is key for full bicep contraction.
• Keep upper arms stationary throughout.

exercises.name: Hammer Curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Hold dumbbells at your sides with a neutral (palms facing in) grip.
2. Curl the weights up keeping palms facing each other the entire time.
3. Squeeze at the top.
4. Lower under control.
exercises.tips:
• Targets the brachialis and brachioradialis in addition to biceps.
• Builds forearm and outer arm thickness.
• Can be done seated, standing, or alternating.

exercises.name: Concentration Curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit on a bench, lean forward with one elbow braced against your inner thigh.
2. Hold a dumbbell with arm fully extended.
3. Curl the weight up toward your shoulder, squeezing hard.
4. Lower slowly.
exercises.tips:
• The braced position eliminates all momentum.
• Great for building the bicep peak.
• Focus on a slow eccentric (3 seconds).

exercises.name: Preacher Curl
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit at a preacher bench with your upper arms flat on the pad.
2. Grip a barbell, EZ bar, or dumbbells with an underhand grip.
3. Curl the weight up, keeping your arms pressed into the pad.
4. Lower slowly to a full stretch.
exercises.tips:
• Don't slam to full extension at the bottom — control it.
• Isolates the short head of the biceps.
• Great for eliminating cheating from curls.

exercises.name: Preacher Curl Machine
exercises.instructions:
1. Sit at the machine, place your upper arms on the pad.
2. Grip the handles.
3. Curl up, squeezing your biceps.
4. Lower slowly under control.
exercises.tips:
• Machine keeps tension constant through the full range.
• Great for dropsets and high-rep finishers.
• Adjust the seat so your armpits rest at the top of the pad.

exercises.name: Skullcrusher
exercises.instructions:
1. Lie on a flat bench holding an EZ bar or dumbbells above your chest.
2. Keeping upper arms still, bend your elbows to lower the weight toward your forehead.
3. Stop just above your forehead (or behind your head for more stretch).
4. Extend your arms to press back up.
exercises.tips:
• Letting the bar go behind your head increases the stretch on the long head.
• Don't flare elbows — keep them pointing at the ceiling.
• Start light to protect your elbows.

exercises.name: Tricep Pushdown
exercises.instructions:
1. Face a cable machine, grip the bar or rope with elbows at your sides.
2. Press the handle down until your arms are fully extended.
3. Squeeze your triceps hard at the bottom.
4. Let the handle come back up to about 90° elbow bend.
exercises.tips:
• Keep your elbows pinned to your sides — don't let them flare.
• A rope lets you spread at the bottom for extra contraction.
• Great for targeting the lateral head of the triceps.

exercises.name: Overhead Tricep Extension
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand or sit, hold a dumbbell, cable, or EZ bar overhead with arms extended.
2. Lower the weight behind your head by bending your elbows.
3. Feel the stretch in your triceps.
4. Extend your arms back to the starting position.
exercises.tips:
• Overhead position stretches the long head of the triceps.
• Keep elbows pointing forward, not flaring out.
• Cable version provides more constant tension.

exercises.name: Cable Crunch
exercises.instructions:
1. Kneel in front of a high cable with a rope attachment.
2. Hold the rope at the sides of your head.
3. Crunch down by flexing your abs, bringing your elbows toward your knees.
4. Return slowly to the starting position.
exercises.tips:
• Don't pull with your arms — the motion should come from your abs.
• Think of curling your ribcage toward your pelvis.
• Keep your hips stationary.

exercises.name: Hanging Leg Raise
exercises.instructions:
1. Hang from a pull-up bar with a slight arm bend.
2. With legs straight (or slightly bent), raise them until parallel or above.
3. Pause at the top.
4. Lower slowly under control.
exercises.tips:
• Avoid swinging — control the motion.
• Bend knees to make it easier, straighten legs to make it harder.
• Curl your pelvis up at the top for maximum ab engagement.

exercises.name: Ab Wheel Rollout
exercises.instructions:
1. Kneel on the floor with the ab wheel in front of you.
2. Grip the handles and roll forward, extending your body.
3. Go as far as you can while maintaining a neutral spine.
4. Use your abs to pull yourself back to the starting position.
exercises.tips:
• Don't let your lower back sag — keep your core braced.
• Start with partial range and progress over time.
• Advanced: do these from your feet instead of knees.

exercises.name: Plank
exercises.instructions:
1. Get on your forearms and toes, body in a straight line.
2. Brace your core, squeeze your glutes.
3. Hold the position for the prescribed time.
4. Don't let your hips sag or pike up.
exercises.tips:
• Once you can hold 60 seconds, progress to harder variations.
• Think of pulling your elbows toward your toes (don't move them).
• Breathe normally throughout — don't hold your breath.

exercises.name: Pallof Press
exercises.instructions:
1. Stand sideways to a cable machine, handle at chest height.
2. Hold the handle at your chest with both hands.
3. Press the handle straight out in front of you, resisting the rotation.
4. Bring it back to your chest. Complete reps, then switch sides.
exercises.tips:
• Your core is working to resist rotation — anti-rotation exercise.
• Keep your hips and shoulders square to the front.
• Great for building functional core stability.

exercises.name: Sled Push
exercises.instructions:
1. Load the sled and grip the high or low handles.
2. Lean into the sled at about a 45° body angle.
3. Drive through your legs, taking powerful steps.
4. Push for the prescribed distance or time.
exercises.tips:
• Low handle pushes are more quad/glute intensive.
• High handle pushes are easier and more upright.
• Great for conditioning with minimal eccentric stress.

exercises.name: Sled Pull
exercises.instructions:
1. Attach a strap to the sled and yourself (hips or hands).
2. Face away from the sled.
3. Walk forward, dragging the sled behind you.
4. Continue for the prescribed distance or time.
exercises.tips:
• Walking backward targets quads more.
• Walking forward targets posterior chain.
• Very low injury risk — no eccentric loading.

exercises.name: Farmer Carry
exercises.instructions:
1. Pick up a heavy dumbbell or implement in each hand.
2. Stand tall with shoulders back and core braced.
3. Walk with short, controlled steps for the prescribed distance.
4. Set the weights down under control.
exercises.tips:
• Grip strength, core stability, and conditioning all in one.
• Keep your shoulders packed down — don't shrug.
• Walk in a straight line and avoid leaning side to side.

exercises.name: Battle Rope
exercises.instructions:
1. Hold one end of the rope in each hand, stand with feet shoulder-width.
2. Alternate slamming the ropes up and down creating waves.
3. Keep your core tight and maintain a slight squat position.
4. Continue for the prescribed time or reps.
exercises.tips:
• Try different patterns: alternating waves, double slams, circles.
• Power comes from your whole body, not just your arms.
• Excellent for HIIT-style conditioning.
